coredata.dc:title: Use of microalgal lipids and carbohydrates for the synthesis of carbon dots via hydrothermal microwave treatment
coredata.dc:creator: Gusain, Deepak
Renuka, Nirmal
Guldhe, Abhishek
Bux, Faizal
coredata.dc:description: The green synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from renewable resources has attracted attention of the scientific community for their use in different industrial applications. CDs derived from microbial biomass can be synthesized using simple and low-cost methods, and have been reported to possess high compatibility, excellent optical behavior and low toxicity. In this study, carbon dots were synthesized using lipids and carbohydrates extracted from microalga Acutodesmus obliquus via microwave hydrothermal digestion method. The synthesized carbon dots showed excitation peaks in the ultraviolet light region (c.a. 350 nm) and emission in the visible region (c.a. 420 nm). The addition of acetone led to red shifting of excitation wavelength in all the synthesized carbon dots. The transmission electron microscopy analysis depicts the nano-size (1.2–11 nm) range of the synthesized carbon dots, where excitation and emission wavelengths were not dependent on the size of the particles. Carbon dots prepared from microalgal metabolites showed fluorescence under ultraviolet luminescence in different wavelengths after the addition of chemicals. The carbon dots thus generated can be explored for their applications such as dyes, sensors, bioimaging and photosensitizers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green synthesis
Nanoparticles
Electron microscopy
Magnetic properties
Microalgae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Extraction of the carbohydrate from microalgae and CDs synthesis (F21)
Extraction of the lipids from microalgae and carbon dots synthesis (F23)
Addition of acetone and analysis of synthesized carbon dots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Deepak Gusain is highly thankful to Durban University of Technology for the award of postdoctoral fellowship. The authors hereby acknowledge the South African Research Chairs Initiative of the Department of Science and Technology and the National Research Foundation (UID: 84166) of South Africa. The authors are highly thankful for characterization facilities provided by Microscopy and microanalysis unit UKZN (TEM). We would like to thank Mr. Subashen Naidu (Microscopy and microanalysis unit UKZN), Luveshan Ramanna and Kriveshin Pillay (IWWT, Durban University of Technology) for their kind assistance.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Samples under normal light (A and C) and under UV light (B and D). A: Samples F21, F22, F23, F25, F27 from right to left; B: Samples F21, F22, F23, F25, F27 from left to right; C: Samples F41, F43, F45, F47 from right to left; D: Samples F41, F43, F45, F47 from left to right. (Table 1).
Fluorescence spectra of sample F21 (A), F23 (B) and F25 (C) (Table 1).
Fluorescence spectra of sample F41 (A), F43 (B), F45 (C) and F47 (D) (Table 1).
TEM (Transmission electron microscopy) images of the synthesized carbon dots (F21, F22, F23, F25 and F27: Table 1).
Different treatments used for the synthesis of carbon dots.

row.entry: Sample code after the addition of acetone
Lipid + hexane + sodium dodecyl sulphate (SDS) + 220 °C for 1 h (microwave)
Lipid + hexane + Chloroform + methanol + 200 °C for 1 h (microwave)

head.ce:title: Use of microalgal lipids and carbohydrates for the synthesis of carbon dots via hydrothermal microwave treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Water and Wastewater Technology, Durban University of Technology, Durban 4000, South Africa

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Water and Wastewater Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Water and Wastewater Technology, Durban University of Technology, Durban 4000, South Africa

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Studies, PGDAV (Evening) College, University of Delhi, New Delhi 110065, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Environmental Studies, PGDAV (Evening) College, University of Delhi, New Delhi 110065, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Amity Institute of Biotechnology, Amity University, Mumbai 410206, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Amity Institute of Biotechnology, Amity University, Mumbai 410206, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The green synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from renewable resources has attracted attention of the scientific community for their use in different industrial applications. CDs derived from microbial biomass can be synthesized using simple and low-cost methods, and have been reported to possess high compatibility, excellent optical behavior and low toxicity. In this study, carbon dots were synthesized using lipids and carbohydrates extracted from microalga Acutodesmus obliquus via microwave hydrothermal digestion method. The synthesized carbon dots showed excitation peaks in the ultraviolet light region (c.a. 350 nm) and emission in the visible region (c.a. 420 nm). The addition of acetone led to red shifting of excitation wavelength in all the synthesized carbon dots. The transmission electron microscopy analysis depicts the nano-size (1.2–11 nm) range of the synthesized carbon dots, where excitation and emission wavelengths were not dependent on the size of the particles. Carbon dots prepared from microalgal metabolites showed fluorescence under ultraviolet luminescence in different wavelengths after the addition of chemicals. The carbon dots thus generated can be explored for their applications such as dyes, sensors, bioimaging and photosensitizers.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) belong to zero-dimensional carbon conformation and are usually quasi-spherical nanoparticles that possess unique properties such as chemical stability, low toxicity, sonorous fluorescence, water dispersibility and biocompatibility. Carbon dots are widely applied for optoelectronics, photocatalysis, bioimaging and sensing applications, where they can be put to use for heavy metal detection [1–4], biosensors [5–7], bio-imaging and fluorescent ink [8,9], tissue engineering [10], light emitting diodes [11], solar cell [12], and catalysis/photocatalysis [13,14]. Various methods have been used to synthesize CDs. However, green approaches using inexpensive raw feedstocks are paying more attention to the scientific community in order to reduce the use of hazardous chemicals [6,10,15]. Previously carbon dots have been synthesized by a number of biological materials such as orange juice [16], sweet pepper [17], capsicum [18], watermelon peel [19], milk [20] via various routes.

In the last two decades, microalgae have received great attention as renewable feedstock for different commercial applications such as feed in aquaculture [21] and poultry [22], treatment of skin disorders [23], as an alternative to chemical fertilizers [24], ingredients for pharmaceutical industry [25], substrate for biodiesel [26], biohydrogen [27] and bioethanol production [28]. Microalgal biomass is also rich resource of many valuable products e.g. lutein [29], astaxanthin [30], β-carotene [31], zeaxanthin [32], polyunsaturated fatty acids (docohexaneoic acid and eicosapentaneoic acid) [33], lycopene [34]. However, limited literature is available in the conversion of algal feedstocks to carbon dots [35]. Thus, it would be prudent to evaluate the potential of microalgal feedstocks for high-value products such as CDs. Algae consist of both hydrophobic (e.g. lipids) and hydrophilic (e.g. carbohydrate) components in their cells. Different chemicals such as 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA) [36], diethylamine (DEA) [36], 1,4- butanediamine (BDA) [36], trimethylamine (TEA) [36] and poly(ethylenemine), ethaloamine [37], diethanolamine [38], ethylenediamine [39], triethylenetetramine [40] are generally used as N (nitrogen) doping agent or N containing functional group into carbon dots to improve the quantum yield [36,41]. Among these, urea is the most commonly used N-doping agent and reported to improve quantum yield. Additionally, the synthesis of nanoparticles using simple hydrothermal processes is generally time-consuming [42–44]. Microwave irradiation has been found to assist in reducing the time for synthesis of carbon dots [37,38,45,46]. Therefore, the objective of the present study was to evaluate the potential of various components (carbohydrate and lipid) of microalga Acutodesmus obliquus for the synthesis of CDs via microwave hydrothermal digestion method, and analyze the optical properties of the synthesized CDs.

The carbon dots were synthesized with carbohydrate and lipid in Microwave digestor (Milestone, START D microwave digestion system). A spectrophotometer (SpectroquantPharo 300, Merck) was used for carbohydrate quantification. Freeze dryer (Mini Lyotrap, LTE Scientific Ltd., United Kingdom) and hot air oven were also used during the procedures. Analytical grade (MERCK South Africa) chemicals (H2SO4, urea, chloroform, methanol, hexane, sodium dodecyl sulphate and acetone) were utilized for experimentation. Synthesized carbon dots were characterized with transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL 2100 HR TEM, Japan) and Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrofluorometer equipped with a Xenon flash lamp (15 W, spectral distribution between 190 nm and 2000 nm) (Agilent, SA).

Freshwater microalga Acutodesmus obliquus [47] was cultivated in 10 m2 raceway pond in standard BG-11 medium in a greenhouse. The average day light intensity and temperature during the cultivation period were 370 µ mol m−2 s−1 and 25 ± 5 °C respectively. Biomass was harvested at the late log phase (12th day of cultivation) using a centrifuge, washed, lyophilized and used for further experimentation and analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of the carbohydrate from microalgae and CDs synthesis (F21)
ce:section.ce:para: Extraction of carbohydrates was carried out with a modified method as suggested by Herenandez et al., 2015. 0.15 g of powdered freeze-dried microalgal biomass was put into 50 mL of 4% H2SO4. The solution was then divided into two parts (25 mL) and placed into the microwave which was kept at 125 °C for a period of 30 min.

Total carbohydrates were quantified using the phenol–sulfuric acid method [48]. Dried biomass was mixed with sulfuric acid (2% v/v) and autoclaved for 30 min at 121 °C for hydrolysis. The mixture was then neutralized with 1 M NaOH/H2SO4. The supernatant was collected by centrifugation at 1509g for 10 min. 0.1 mL of supernatant was diluted to 1 mL, and then mixed with 1 mL of phenol (5% w/v) and 5 mL of 96% H2SO4. After cooling to 25–30 °C, the absorbance of this solution was measured at 490 nm using a spectrophotometer (SpectroquantPharo300, Merck). Total carbohydrates were quantified referring to a calibration curve prepared using glucose as a standard.

For the synthesis of CDs, extracted carbohydrates solution was removed and centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was collected, and 20 mL of supernatant was mixed with the urea solution (6 g in 40 mL). Urea was used as a nitrogen (N) doping agent along with carbon source [36,41]. The mixture was stirred for 10 min and afterwards was subjected to the microwave at 180 °C for 60 min. The brown colour solution (allegedly synthesized carbon dots) was then characterized with different techniques (Fluorometer and TEM analysis).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of the lipids from microalgae and carbon dots synthesis (F23)
ce:section.ce:para: Extraction of lipids was carried out with a modified method of Bligh and Dyer [49,50]. In order to extract the lipid, 0.25 g of the freeze-dried microalgal biomass was mixed in 50 mL of water. The suspension was poured into a solution of chloroform and methanol (2: 1 v:v) and stirred for 5 min. The mixture (freeze-dried microalgal biomass + water + chloroform + methanol) was divided into parts and microwaved at 100 °C for 10 min. The sample (after microwave) was then filtered to remove debris. Organic layer was separated and oven dried at 70 °C for gravimetric quantification of lipid content.

For the synthesis of carbon dots, hexane was poured into a beaker containing extracted lipid filtrate, which led to a mixture of partial material and hexane (due to solubility of material in hexane). The hexane soluble mixture was then mixed with urea solution (6 g in 40 mL) and put into a microwave oven at 220 °C, which resulted in two layers, i.e. hexane and aqueous layer. The aqueous layer (allegedly synthesized carbon dots) was separated and used for further characterization (Flourometer and TEM analysis). Various sample preparations are represented in Table 1 .

The synthesis of sample F25 is similar to sample F23 except that in F25, sodium dodecyl sulphate solution was added in hexane and urea solution in the last stage prior to microwave at 220 °C. The synthesis of sample F27 was also similar to F23 synthesis, except that in F27, chloroform and methanol (10 mL in ratio of 2: 1) solution was added in hexane and urea solution in the last stage prior to microwave at 220 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Addition of acetone and analysis of synthesized carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The aqueous layer material (allegedly containing carbon dots) was mixed with acetone and kept for three days. This led to the change in colour of the sample (F21 to F27) from orange to light red (F41 to F47). The spectrofluorometric analysis of the sample was carried out afterwards.

Various sample preparations are represented in Table 1. The urea from the synthesized samples (F21 and F23 to F27) was removed prior to TEM analysis. To remove urea, the samples (F21 and F23 to F27) were poured into acetone (1:3 v: v). Afterwards, the samples were kept for 24 h at the ambient temperature. The samples were then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was collected, and dried in an oven at 70 °C for 6 h. After drying, the solid residue was then mixed with water, and used for TEM analysis.

In the present study, microalga A. obliquus biomass grown in a greenhouse contained 35 ± 1.97% and 18 ± 2% of carbohydrates and lipids on dry biomass basis respectively. Lipid or carbohydrate fraction of the microalgal biomass were used as carbon source for the synthesis of CDs using an external nitrogen source such as urea. However, carbonized carbon could not be used as a carbon source for CDs synthesis, this can be established by the non-fluorescence behavior of sample F22 (carbonized carbon; Fig. 1 B). Findings of the present study showed that the source of carbon i.e. carbohydrates or lipids fraction, and addition of sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) during the synthesis of lipid derived carbon dots did not show significant impact on the color of C-dots under UV light (Fig. 1). This can be attributed to independent relation of degree of surface oxidation or independence of generated defined band gaps on the source of carbon from algae.

The possible mechanism is similar to the majority of carbon dots synthesized previously [37,38]. The microwave synthesis may help in the faster carbonization as compared to conventional carbonization [45]. The lipids and carbohydrates extracted from the microalgae undergo dehydration, carbonization and crosslinking leading to carbon particles of nanometre size. While, urea can act as a source of carbon and nitrogen along with a passivating agent. Being acting as passivating agents, it helps in the incorporation of nitrogen onto the surface of carbon dots, which may assist in an increase in quantum yield of the synthesized CDs.

Sample synthesized with carbohydrates fraction were analyzed under visible and UV light (365 nm) (Figs. 2 and 3
). The absorption band in all the tested samples was in the wide range in the UV–visible region. The broad range adsorption was attributed to the broad range distribution of the carbon dots [5,19]. The first excitation peak in all samples was in the UV region (F21 = 354 nm, F23 = 343 nm and F25 = 337 nm). The corresponding emission peak was in visible region (F21 = 430 nm, F23 = 422 nm and F25 = 413 nm). The second excitation was also observed in all the samples (F21 = 630 nm, F23 = 624 nm and F25 = 624 nm). However, the peak of sample F25 was broad and not sharp as compared to sample F21 and F23. The excitation in the c.a. 350 nm belongs to the n-p* transition of surface groups [51,52] in Fig. 2 (sample F21 and F23 to F25). The dual excitation and dual emission in the spectra can correspond to multiple excited states, which is an unusual property likely in organic molecules [53,54].

The addition of SDS during the synthesis of lipid derived carbon dots did not show any significant difference in UV–Visible absorption and emission characteristics as compared to carbohydrates derived carbon dots. However, the synthesized samples on treatment with acetone changed colour from transparent to orange or to dark orange (Fig. 1). In addition to the change in colour, the fluorescence spectra of the samples were also changed (Fig. 3).

The excitation of the samples was shifted from UV to the visible region on the addition of acetone. The emission was also shifted from blue to the green-yellow region (534–552 nm). The excitation peak in different synthesized samples was not clear, however it was in the visible range. In most of the previous studies, the effect of solvent on luminescence was based on the polarity of the solvent [55]. An increase in the polarity of the solvent leads to shifting its emission transmission towards the red region. However, in the present study, change in the colour of CDs from blue to orange, and shift in absorbance and emission was attributed to the change in the degree of surface oxidation. The change in degree of surface oxidation leads to a decrease in the band gap, which further leads to red shifted photoluminescence emission. The effect was more prominent in carbon dots derived from lipid fraction (F23, F25, F27 to F43, F45, F47) as compared to carbohydrates (F21 and F41).

TEM analysis of the synthesized samples suggested the presence of very small particles in the nanoscale (Fig. 4 ). TEM samples are prone to aggregation on the sample grid, due to drying of the sample for the preparation for TEM analysis. The sample F22 (carbonized carbon not carbon dot) showed bigger sizes of particles i.e. 6–12 nm. While, the material, F21 and F23 revealed size from 1.2 to 2.2 nm and 1.5 to 6.6 nm respectively. The sample F45 and F47 showed particle size range of 3.5 to 11 nm and 1.8 to 3.05 nm respectively.

Bao et al. [56] suggested variable emission with the size of the carbon dots, however the current study did not show this kind of behavior with variable particle size in TEM analysis [56]. This can be attributed to the independence of degree of oxidation with the size of samples, and more dependency on the chemical nature (i.e. functional groups present on the surface) of the surface sites.

CDs synthesized in the present study showed particle size under 10 nm, as in most of the studies in the literature with different feedstock (Table 2 ). The shape of the particles was spherical as in most of the studies [65]. Similar to previous studies, a red shift of the spectrum on excitation of carbon dots was observed, which is one of the usual characteristics of carbon dots. In the protic solvents, the high N content and the presence of imine nitrogen are the reason for red emission. However, carbon dots synthesized by pepper follow anti-stokes photoluminescence (Table 2).

In the present study, carbon dots were successfully synthesized from the carbohydrates and lipids extracted from biomass of microalga A. obliquus in the particle size range of 1.2 nm to 11 nm (mostly 2 nm to 6 nm). Synthesized carbon dots depicted noticeable fluorescent behavior under UV radiation. However, the source of carbon (lipids or carbohydrates) from microalgae did not affect the fluorescence behavior of the synthesized carbon dots. It was further demonstrated that the addition of the acetone in the synthesized samples led to red shift in the absorption spectra. The carbon dots thus generated can be further explored in applications such as sensors, in vitro and in vivo imaging, fluorescent ink and photosensitizers. Microalgal metabolites present wide opportunities for the synthesis of a range of high-value products. However, green synthesis of carbon dots from this renewable source is yet to be established fully. Future studies on synthesis, mechanisms, cytotoxicity, and cell permeability would be useful to prove their potential in different sectors.

Deepak Gusain: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Nirmal Renuka: Conceptualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Abhishek Guldhe: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Faizal Bux: Supervision, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Deepak Gusain is highly thankful to Durban University of Technology for the award of postdoctoral fellowship. The authors hereby acknowledge the South African Research Chairs Initiative of the Department of Science and Technology and the National Research Foundation (UID: 84166) of South Africa. The authors are highly thankful for characterization facilities provided by Microscopy and microanalysis unit UKZN (TEM). We would like to thank Mr. Subashen Naidu (Microscopy and microanalysis unit UKZN), Luveshan Ramanna and Kriveshin Pillay (IWWT, Durban University of Technology) for their kind assistance.
